text:
The Richest Man in Babylon

In ancient Babylon, there once lived a very rich man named Arkad. He was famous far and wide for his immense wealth. He was also well-known for his generosity. He gave freely to charities. He was generous with his family, and he spent freely on his own lifestyle. But despite all this, every year his wealth grew much faster than he could spend it.

One day, some friends from his youth came to him and said,

Arkad, you are much more fortunate than we are. You have become the richest man in all of Babylon, while we still struggle just to survive. You can wear the finest clothes. You can enjoy the rarest foods. Meanwhile, we have to be satisfied if we can simply keep our families decently dressed and fed as best we can.

We were once equals. We studied under the same teacher, and we played the same games. You didn't outshine us in our studies or in our games. And in the years since then, you have not been a better citizen than we have.

As far as we can tell, you haven't worked harder or more faithfully, either. So why did a fickle fate single you out to enjoy all the good things in life, while completely ignoring us when we deserve just as much?
Arkad gently corrected them, saying,
If you have barely scraped by since our younger days, it is for one of two reasons. Either you have failed to learn the laws that control the building of wealth, or you are simply not following them.

This 'fickle fate' you speak of is a vicious goddess. She never brings permanent good to anyone. In fact, she brings ruin to almost every person she showers with unearned gold. She creates reckless spenders. They quickly waste everything they receive. Then, they are left with overwhelming appetites and desires that they can no longer afford to satisfy. Others who receive her favors become misers. They hoard their wealth because they are terrified of spending it. They know they don't have the skills to replace what they spend. They are also paralyzed by the fear of robbers. They doom themselves to lives of emptiness and secret misery.

There are probably others who can take unearned gold, add to it, and continue to live as happy, contented citizens. But there are so few of them that I only know of them through rumors. Just think about the men you know who have inherited sudden wealth, and ask yourselves if these things are not true.
His friends admitted that this was exactly what happened to the men they knew who had inherited wealth. They begged Arkad to explain how he had managed to build so much prosperity. So, he continued:

When I was young, I looked around me. I saw all the good things that could bring happiness and contentment. I realized that wealth made all of these things much more powerful. Wealth is power. With wealth, many things become possible.
You can decorate your home with the richest furnishings. You can sail across distant seas.
You can feast on the delicacies of faraway lands.
You can buy ornaments from the gold worker and the stone polisher.
You can even build mighty temples for the Gods.
You can do all these things, and many more. These things delight the senses and deeply satisfy the soul.

When I realized all of this, I made a decision. I decided that I would claim my share of the good things in life. I refused to be someone who just stands on the sidelines, enviously watching others enjoy themselves. I refused to be satisfied with wearing the cheapest clothes that looked barely respectable. I refused to settle for the life of a poor man. Instead, I decided to make myself a guest at this banquet of good things.

As you know, I was the son of a humble merchant. I came from a large family with no hope of an inheritance. And as you so frankly pointed out, I was not blessed with any superior intelligence or special talents. I decided that achieving my desires would require two things: time and study.

As for time, all people have plenty of it. Every single one of you has let enough time slip by to have made yourselves wealthy. Yet, as you admit, you have nothing to show for it except your good families, which you can be justly proud of.

As for study, do you remember what our wise teacher taught us? He said that learning comes in two forms. The first form is the knowledge we learn and memorize. The second form is the training that teaches us how to discover the things we do not know.

Therefore, I decided to find out exactly how a person could accumulate wealth. Once I found the answer, I made it my personal mission to do it, and to do it well. After all, isn't it wise for us to enjoy life while we are still standing in the bright sunshine? We will have plenty of time for sorrow when we pass into the darkness of the spirit world.

I found a job working as a scribe in the hall of records. Every day, I worked long hours carving on clay tablets. I labored week after week, and month after month. But I had absolutely nothing to show for all my earnings. Food, clothing, offerings to the gods, and other things I can't even remember simply swallowed up everything I made. But my determination never faded.

Then, one day, Algamish the money lender came to the house of the city master. He ordered a copy of the Ninth Law. He told me, 'I need this in two days. If the job is finished by then, I will give you two copper coins.'

So I worked incredibly hard, but the law was very long. When Algamish returned, the job was still unfinished. He was furious. If I had been his slave, he would have beaten me. But I knew the city master would not let him hurt me, so I wasn't afraid. I looked at him and said, 'Algamish, you are a very wealthy man. Tell me how I can become rich too. In return, I will carve on this clay all night long. By the time the sun rises, it will be completely finished.'
He smiled at me and replied, 'You are a bold rascal, but we have a deal.'

I carved all through the night. My back ached, and the smell of the burning wick gave me a terrible headache. My eyes hurt so much I could barely see. But when he returned at sunrise, the clay tablets were completely finished.
'Now,' I said, 'tell me what you promised.'

'You have kept your end of the bargain, my son,' he said to me kindly. 'And I am ready to keep mine. I will tell you the things you want to know. I am growing old, and an old tongue loves to talk. When a young person comes to an older one for advice, he receives the wisdom of many years. Unfortunately, young people often think that the elderly only know the wisdom of the past, so they fail to profit from it. But you must remember this: the sun that shines today is the exact same sun that shone when your father was born. And it will still be shining when your very last grandchild passes into the darkness.

'The thoughts of youth,' he continued, 'are like bright lights that flash like meteors across the sky. But the wisdom of age is like the fixed stars. They shine so steadily and constantly that a sailor can depend on them to steer his ship.
'Pay close attention to my words. If you don't, you will fail to grasp the truth I am about to share, and you will believe that all your hard work last night was for nothing.'
Then he looked at me shrewdly from under his shaggy brows. He spoke in a low, forceful tone. 'I found the road to wealth when I decided that a part of all I earned was mine to keep. And so will you.'
Then he continued to look at me with a piercing glance, but he said no more.
'Is that all?' I asked.
'That was enough to change the heart of a sheep herder into the heart of a money lender,' he replied.
'But all I earn is mine to keep, isn't it?' I demanded.

'Far from it,' he replied. 'Do you not pay the garment-maker? Do you not pay the sandal-maker? Do you not pay for the things you eat? Can you live in Babylon without spending? What do you have to show for your earnings of the past month? What about the past year? Fool! You pay everyone but yourself. You are dull, laboring only for others. You might as well be a slave, working just for what your master gives you to eat and wear. If you kept one-tenth of all you earned for yourself, how much would you have in ten years?'
My knowledge of numbers did not fail me. I answered, 'As much as I earn in one year.'

'You speak only half the truth,' he retorted. '**Every gold piece you save becomes a slave to work for you.** Every copper it earns is its child that can also earn for you. If you want to become wealthy, what you save must earn money. Its children must also earn money. Then they will all help to give you the abundance you crave.'
'You think I am cheating you for your long night's work,' he continued. 'But I am paying you a thousand times over. You just need the intelligence to grasp the truth I offer you.'

'**A part of all you earn is yours to keep.** It should be no less than a tenth, no matter how little you earn. It can be more if you can afford it. Pay yourself first. Do not buy more from the clothes-maker and the sandal-maker than you can afford out of the rest. You must still have enough left for food, charity, and penance to the gods.'

'Wealth, like a tree, grows from a tiny seed. The first copper you save is the seed from which your tree of wealth will grow. The sooner you plant that seed, the sooner the tree will grow. The more faithfully you nourish and water that tree with consistent savings, the sooner you can bask in contentment beneath its shade.'
Saying this, he took his clay tablets and went away.

I thought a lot about what he told me. It seemed reasonable, so I decided to try it. Each time I was paid, I took one out of every ten copper pieces and hid it away. Strange as it may seem, I was not any shorter on funds than before. I noticed very little difference, and I managed to get along without that money. But as my hoard began to grow, I was often tempted. I wanted to spend it on the good things the merchants displayed. These goods were brought by camels and ships from the land of the Phoenicians. However, I wisely held back.
A full year after Algamish had gone, he returned and spoke to me. 'Son, have you paid yourself at least one-tenth of all you earned for the past year?'
I answered proudly, 'Yes, master, I have.' 'That is good,' he answered, beaming upon me. 'And what have you done with it?'

'I gave it to Azmur, the brickmaker. He told me he was traveling over the far seas. He said he would buy the rare jewels of the Phoenicians for me in Tyre. When he returns, we will sell these at high prices and divide the earnings.'

'Every fool must learn,' he growled. 'But why trust a brickmaker's knowledge about jewels? Would you go to the breadmaker to ask about the stars? No, by my tunic, you would go to the astrologer if you had the power to think. Your savings are gone, young man. You have yanked your wealth-tree up by the roots. But plant another one and try again. Next time, if you want advice about jewels, go to the jewel merchant. If you want to know the truth about sheep, go to the herdsman. Advice is one thing that is freely given away, but be careful to only take what is worth having. Anyone who takes advice about his savings from someone inexperienced will pay with those very savings to prove their opinions false.' Saying this, he went away.

And it happened exactly as he said. The Phoenicians are scoundrels. They sold Azmur worthless bits of glass that looked like gems. But just as Algamish had told me, I started saving every tenth copper again. I had formed the habit, so it was no longer difficult.
Twelve months later, Algamish came back to the room of the scribes and spoke to me. 'What progress have you made since I last saw you?'
'I have paid myself faithfully,' I replied. 'I entrusted my savings to Agger the shieldmaker to buy bronze. Every fourth month, he pays me rental interest.'

'That is good. And what do you do with the rental interest?' 'I throw a great feast with honey, fine wine, and spiced cake. I also bought myself a scarlet tunic. Someday, I will buy a young donkey to ride.' Algamish laughed at this. 'You are eating the children of your savings. How do you expect them to work for you then? And how can they have children that will also work for you? First, get yourself an army of golden slaves. After that, you can enjoy many rich banquets without any regret.' Saying this, he went away again.

I did not see him again for two years. When he finally returned, his face was full of deep lines and his eyes drooped. He was becoming a very old man. He said to me, 'Arkad, have you achieved the wealth you dreamed of yet?'
And I answered, 'Not yet all that I desire. But I have some, and it earns more money, and its earnings earn even more.'
'And do you still take the advice of brickmakers?'
'About brickmaking, they give good advice,' I retorted.
Arkad,
he continued,

you have learned your lessons well. First, you learned to live on less than you earn. Next, you learned to seek advice only from those who are qualified through their own experience to give it. Finally, you have learned to make gold work for you.

You have taught yourself how to acquire money, how to keep it, and how to use it. Because of this, you are ready for a position of true responsibility. I am growing old. My sons think only of spending and give no thought to earning money. My business interests are massive, and I fear they are becoming too much for me to manage. If you will go to Nippur and look after my lands there, I will make you my partner. You will share in my estate.

So I went to Nippur and took charge of his massive holdings. Because I was full of ambition, and because I had mastered the three laws of successfully handling wealth, I was able to greatly increase the value of his properties.
I prospered immensely. When the spirit of Algamish finally departed for the sphere of darkness, I shared in his estate exactly as he had arranged under the law.
So spoke Arkad. When he finished his story, one of his friends spoke up,
You were certainly lucky that Algamish made you his heir.
I was only lucky in one respect,
Arkad replied.

I had the desire to prosper long before I ever met him. Didn't I prove my clear sense of purpose for four years by keeping one-tenth of everything I earned? Would you call a fisherman lucky if he spent years studying the habits of the fish? He studies them so well that with each changing wind, he knows exactly where to cast his nets. Opportunity is a proud goddess. She wastes no time with those who are unprepared.
You had strong willpower to keep going after you lost your first year's savings. You are unusual in that way,
another friend said.
Willpower!
Arkad retorted.

What nonsense. Do you think willpower gives a man the strength to lift a burden that a camel cannot carry? Can it help him pull a load that even oxen cannot move? Willpower is simply an unflinching purpose. It is the determination to fulfill a task you have set for yourself. If I set a task for myself, no matter how small, I will see it through. How else could I ever have the confidence to do important things? Suppose I say to myself, 'For a hundred days, as I walk across the bridge into the city, I will pick a pebble from the road and throw it into the stream.' If I say it, I will do it. If I cross the bridge on the seventh day and forget, I will not say, 'Tomorrow I will throw two pebbles, and that will be just as good.' Instead, I will turn around, retrace my steps, and cast that single pebble. On the twentieth day, I will not say, 'Arkad, this is useless. What is the point of throwing a pebble every day? Just throw a handful right now and be done with it.' No, I would never say that, and I would never do it. When I set a task for myself, I finish it. Because of this, I am careful not to start difficult or impractical tasks. After all, I love my leisure time.
Then another friend spoke up.
If what you tell us is true, it certainly sounds reasonable. But since it is so simple, if all men did it, there would not be enough wealth to go around.
Wealth grows wherever men exert energy,
Arkad replied.

If a rich man builds a new palace, is the gold he pays out gone forever? No. The brickmaker gets part of it. The laborer gets part of it. The artist gets part of it. Everyone who works on the house gets a share. And when the palace is finished, isn't it worth all the gold it cost? Isn't the ground it stands on worth more simply because the palace is there? Doesn't the adjoining land also increase in value? Wealth grows in magical ways. No man can predict its limits. Haven't the Phoenicians built great cities on barren coasts, using the wealth that comes from their merchant ships on the seas?
What do you advise us to do, then, so that we may also become rich?
asked still another friend.
The years have passed. We are no longer young men, and we have nothing saved up.

I advise you to take the wisdom of Algamish to heart. Say to yourselves: **'A part of all I earn is mine to keep.'** Say it in the morning when you first wake up. Say it at noon. Say it at night. Say it every hour of every day. Repeat it to yourself until the words stand out like letters of fire across the sky.

Impress yourself with this idea. Fill yourself with the thought. Then, take whatever portion seems wise. Let it be no less than one-tenth of your earnings, and set it aside. Arrange your other expenses around this if necessary. But always set that portion aside first. Soon, you will realize what a rich feeling it is to own a treasure that belongs only to you. As it grows, it will excite you. A new joy for life will thrill you. You will find yourself putting in greater effort to earn more. After all, if your earnings increase, won't you also get to keep a larger portion for yourself?
Then, learn to make your treasure work for you. Make it your slave. Make its children and its children's children work for you.

Ensure a steady income for your future. Look at the elderly around you, and do not forget that one day, you will be numbered among them. Therefore, invest your treasure with the greatest caution so that it is not lost. Promises of unusually high returns are like deceitful sirens. They sing only to lure the unwary onto the rocks of loss and regret.

Also, provide for your family so they will not be left in need if the Gods call you to their realms. You can always secure this protection by making small payments at regular intervals. A wise and prepared man does not wait around, hoping for a large sum of money to suddenly appear for such a noble purpose.

Consult with wise men. Seek the advice of men whose daily work is handling money. Let them save you from the kind of mistake I made when I trusted my money to the judgment of Azmur, the brickmaker. A small but safe return is far more desirable than taking a foolish risk.

Enjoy life while you are here. Do not overstrain yourself or try to save too much. If saving one-tenth of your earnings is all you can comfortably manage, be content to keep that portion. Otherwise, live according to your income. Do not become stingy or afraid to spend your money. Life is good, and it is rich with worthwhile things to enjoy.

His friends thanked him and walked away. Some remained silent because they lacked imagination and simply could not understand. Some were sarcastic, believing that a man as rich as Arkad should share his wealth with his less fortunate old friends. But a few had a new light in their eyes. They realized that Algamish had kept returning to the scribe's room because he was watching a man work his way out of darkness and into the light. When Arkad finally found that light, a place was waiting for him. No one else could fill that position until he had worked out his own understanding and was truly ready for opportunity.

These enlightened few were the ones who frequently revisited Arkad in the years that followed. He always received them gladly. He offered them counsel and freely shared his wisdom, just as men of broad experience are always happy to do. He helped them invest their savings safely, ensuring their money earned good interest without being lost or trapped in investments that paid no dividends.

The turning point in these men's lives happened on the very day they finally understood the truth. It was a truth that had been passed from Algamish to Arkad, and from Arkad directly to them.

**A PART OF ALL YOU EARN IS YOURS TO KEEP.**
